issue_title: Allow WebSocketServerProtocol.onConnect to return a Future/Deferred
stop spamming DIRAC/bad calls
issue_body:
I'm seeing the fact that we're spamming DIRAC with calls such as ```status([])```. These aren't giving us any information we don't know but are taking some time on the Monitoring thread when they're there.

I'm also seeing the Monitoring thread now being _too fast_ and we're finalizing the same job twice. This comes from the fact we don't have some global register about what we've currently put into a queue to be finalized.
I plan to add a global register with objects popped only after they've actually completed for the Dirac backend.

issue_title: MAC possibly allowing non-encrypted packets to be passed off as legit network packets
PEXBuilder does not respect interpreter when compiling source to bytecode
issue_body:
Today when calling `add_source` on a `PEXBuilder`, any '.py' files are byte code compiled using the current ambient interpreter and not the `PythonInterpreter` the `PEXBuilder` was constructed with: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/v1.0.0/pex/pex_builder.py#L136

This leads to problems when the `PEXBuilder` is being used in an interpeter that has language incompatibilites with the target interpreter; ie: when running under 2.7 and trying to contruct a pex targeted to 3.4 using python 3 only language features.  An example of this is outlined in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/1658

issue_title: Add support for "installing" a binary that was created out of band.
issue_body: This could be as simple as `./pants install.binary --path=<prebuilt oob path> --name=<ie: thrift> --version=<version>`.  This would allow both prep_commands and general outside scripts or one-time setup routines (think dev machine imaging scripts), to install a binary into the correct path under `~/.cache/pants/bin/...`.  Aurora could use this to install its own pre-built thrift binary and pants itself could use it to install its native components for its own development workflow (see #3999).

issue_title: NailgunExecutor should more strictly check process information to safeguard against stale metadata reuse in the ProcessManager lifecycle.
issue_body:
A user reported an issue:

```
20:31:48 06:28             [zinc]
                           ==== stderr ====

                           ==== stdout ====

WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -15 3075!
20:29:54 04:34           [compile]
                       compile(src/jvm/com/foursquare/apiserver/api2/services:services) failed: failed to kill pid 3075 with signals (15, 9)WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -9 3075!

20:31:48 06:28             [zinc]
                           ==== stderr ====

                           ==== stdout ====

WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -15 3075!
20:30:03 04:43           [compile]
                       compile(src/jvm/com/foursquare/services/batch:batch) failed: failed to kill pid 3075 with signals (15, 9)WARN] caught OSError([Errno 1] Operation not permitted) during attempt to kill -9 3075!
```

it was determined that nailgun management in pants runs in disparate buildroots may be occasionally colliding due to non-strict checking of process metadata in the instance of stale pids.

we currently pass a parameter to the nailgun cmdline specifying the buildroot. this is checked during an `ng-killall` (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/java/nailgun_executor.py#L41) but not in the inline lifecycle management of NailgunExecutor (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/java/nailgun_executor.py#L162).

the immediate remedy here appears to be to extend this checking throughout.

issue_title: Support a restricted version of the HTML report for security reasons
issue_body:
When Jenkins addressed their [stored XSS vulnerability through workspace files and archived artifacts](https://wiki.jenkins-ci.org/display/SECURITY/Jenkins+Security+Advisory+2015-12-09), the HTML reports served became very limited. While it's possible to relax the security in Jenkins, it's not advisable. I wonder if we could degrade the HTML report gracefully for this case.

These are the [default rules](https://wiki.jenkins-ci.org/display/JENKINS/Configuring+Content+Security+Policy) now imposed by Jenkins:
* No JavaScript allowed at all
* No plugins (object/embed) allowed
* No inline CSS, or CSS from other sites allowed
* No images from other sites allowed
* No frames allowed
* No web fonts allowed
* No XHR/AJAX allowed

Without JavaScript, we lose support for [splitting the log and additional HTML onto a separate row](https://github.com/davehunt/pytest-html/blob/896fc16adbed412d8fd7fd77110096c092ec3124/pytest_html/resources/main.js#L163-L192), the [default sort order of test results](https://github.com/davehunt/pytest-html/blob/896fc16adbed412d8fd7fd77110096c092ec3124/pytest_html/resources/main.js#L126-L132), and the ability for users to change the sort order of the report. We should see if it's possible to take care of the first two in the generated HTML instead of relying on JavaScript.

All CSS is currently inline, so it may make sense for us to generate a CSS file alongside the HTML report. Currently the user specifies the path to the HTML file, which means we either have to make some assumptions about the available name for the CSS file. Perhaps instead of a file, we could support a directory, to which `index.html` and `style.css` can be written.

We also lose the [handling of images](https://github.com/davehunt/pytest-html/blob/896fc16adbed412d8fd7fd77110096c092ec3124/pytest_html/resources/main.js#L126-L132) that prevents large data URLs to be duplicated in the HTML. I believe that using data URLs for images should still work, so we should either find an optimal way to use them for both links and displayed images, or consider saving images as separate resources.

issue_body:
A really useful feature would be to be able to select a random sample of ~100 Examples and see how the model scores them. It could be over time, or just at the current moment.

issue_title: MusicXML PNG: explanation of Environment Keys
issue_body:
When outputting to PNG, I get the ``EnvironmentException`` raised in ``environment.py:L685``. It's because there's no ``environmentKey`` set for ``"musicxml.png"``. That's easy to solve by inserting an ``"elif"`` clause around line 648.

For that matter, I'm not clear why there's a ``"musicxml.png"`` format at all, and especially why LilyPond outputs to that format. A few comments around ``common.fileExtensions`` (lines 32 to 57) would be helpful for that reason. Maybe a brief description of how music21 uses each format.

I wondered which environment setting it should use---probably ``graphicsPath``? Maybe, but I have ``graphicsPath`` set to "gimp" so maybe at some point something said it should be a graphics editing application? The environment module's documentation doesn't say what any of the settings are for, so it's hard to know.

```msg = ('No installers seem to be present and working on your system; '
                   'fix that or try running letsencrypt with the "certonly" command')
msg = ('No installers are available on your OS yet; try running '
                   '"letsencrypt-auto certonly" to get a cert you can install manually')```

I think the message is not clear as it is using the term "Installers" which might not be immediately apparent to be a cert installer. I'm not the only one who got confused by it, for example:
https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/no-installers-seem-to-be-present-and-working-on-your-system/1791

I suggest changing it to something like:
"Couldn't install certificate on your server. No cert installers were found on your system. Fix that or run letsencrypt with the "certonly" command to get a certificate and install in manually."

issue_title: Exponential growth in time required to run large numbers of tests
issue_body:
I'm still investigating the root cause of this issue, but it is reproducible.

I discovered this problem while working on https://github.com/tomcatmanager/tomcatmanager, a project which utilizes `cmd2`. A complete run of the test suite for that project makes several hundred calls to `cmd2.Cmd.onecmd_plus_hooks()`.  As the test suite has grown, I have noticed the performance of the test suite degrading in a non-linear way. I finally decided to try and track down why.

I discovered that as the number of calls to `cmd2.Cmd.onecmd_plus_hooks()` increases in a test run, the time required to execute each call grows very quickly. It's easy to duplicate using the test suite in `cmd2`. First, install the `pytest-repeat` module, which allows you to repeat each test in a suite any number of times.

Then run:
```
$ pytest tests/test_argparse.py -v --duration=0
```

On my computer those 18 tests run in less than 1 second. However, in the duration report at the end, which shows the clock time required for each test to run, you can see that some of the tests completed in 0.01 seconds, and others took 0.09 seconds. None of the tests in that file are an order of magnitude more expensive to run than the others, but you could maybe chalk it up to variation in what else is running on your computer.

Now try:
```
$ pytest tests/test_argparse.py -v --duration=0 --count=10
```
This will run each test 10 times. I expect that the time to run the tests would grow linearly with the number of iterations, so it should take perhaps 10 seconds to complete the run. Go ahead and go make some tea, because on my computer it took 464 seconds (almost 8 minutes!) to complete.

Examine the duration report at the end of the test run. Here's what I saw as the 10 slowest tests:
```
============================ slowest test durations ============================
9.28s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[8/10]
9.26s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[4/10]
9.20s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[10/10]
9.19s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[7/10]
9.17s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[9/10]
9.02s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[2/10]
8.78s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[1/10]
8.78s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[5/10]
8.76s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[3/10]
8.68s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[6/10]
```

Something has got to be broken, because there is no way it should take more than 9 seconds to run one of those tests.

When I made this discovery on https://github.com/tomcatmanager/tomcatmanager, I assumed there was some problem with the networking test code I had created, so I profiled the test runs and examined the output. The growth in clock time for the tests all occurs in the `pyparsing` module.

Using `pytest-xdist` seems to mask the problem, especially if you distribute the tests to a large number of workers. For example, if you use 16 workers, each worker will end up running approximately one sixteenth of the tests, and for "medium" sized test suites that would be enough to reduce the number of calls each worker makes to `pyparsing` so that the clock time required for each test doesn't begin to grow.

The `pytest-forked` module allows you to run each test in a new forked process. This prevents memory leaks or cache errors in one test from affecting other tests in the suite. If you install `pytest-forked` and run:
```
$ pytest tests/test_argparse.py -v --duration=0 --count=10 --forked
```

The test suite finishes 10 runs of each test (on my computer) in less than 9 seconds, which fits with the linear growth of test time I would expect.

At this point I think the likely culprit is the construction of the `cmd2` grammar for pyparsing, but it could also be a bug in pyparsing. More investigation definitely required. The good news is that this isn't super urgent, because there is a work around with `--forked`.

issue_title: Remove num_workers from the ray.init API.
issue_body:
Users should specify the quantity of **resources** (as opposed to workers) available to the system via `num_cpus=` and `num_gpus=` when calling `ray.init()` (by default `num_cpus=psutil.cpu_count()` and `num_gpus=0`).

The quantity of these resources will determine how many tasks the local scheduler will try to execute in parallel. The local scheduler will maintain a pool of workers, but the number of workers won't rate limit the number of tasks that are executing in parallel. If more workers are needed, the local scheduler will start more workers. If there are more than enough workers, the local scheduler will leave some of them idle.

issue_title: Remove the import thread from the workers and driver.
issue_body:
Right now the import thread fetches remote function definitions, actor definitions, custom class definitions (needed for custom serialization callbacks). It should be possible to get rid of the extra thread and to simply fetch these values from Redis when they are needed (though that could add latency the first time a remote function is invoked on a worker).

This would also address an issue brought up in #824.

issue_title: Make ExaBGP fit for draft-ietf-idr-rfc5575bis-01
issue_body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
 - Feature Idea

##### SUMMARY
<!--- Explain the problem briefly -->
I promised to write a short summary of what I think is needed to make ExaBGP fit for RFC5575bis. Note that draft-ietf-idr-rfc5575bis-01 is only draft status. However the required changes should not break compatibility with existing implementations.

### My Summary:

(I am referring to the current draft-ietf-idr-rfc5575bis-01 section numbers)

**Section 4.2.3**

The possible comparision bit combinations for the numeric operator (lt, gt, eq)
have been explicitly defined (RFC 5575 leaves this open). 

ExaBGP may need to add !=, true, false.   

**Section 4.2.3, 4.2.4, 4.2.5, 4.2.6, 4.2.7, 4.2.8, 4.2.10, 4.2.11**

Those sections (flow components) use the exact same numeric operator. The
allowed length (len) of the value is defined in each of those sections:
```
4.2.3 Type3-IP Protocol = 1 byte (len = 00) 
4.2.4 Type4-Port = 1 or 2 byte 
4.2.5 Type5-Dest-Port = 1 or 2 byte 
4.2.6 Type6-Src-Port = 1 or 2 byte 
4.2.7 ICMP-Type =1 byte 
4.2.8 ICMP-Code = 1 byte 
4.2.10 Packet-Length = 1 or 2 byte 
4.2.11 DSCP = 1 byte
```
ExaBGP may need to encode BGP-flow-updates with the correct length.  This is
completely compatible with RFC5575 only that the use of the length field is  now
more restrictive.

**Section 4.2.9, 4.2.12**

Those sections (flow components) use the same bitmask operator defined in 4.2.9

**Section 7 (and 7.2)**

A new traffic filtering action (traffic-rate-packets) has been introduced.

Since there is no ext-community assigned yet there is nothing to do.

**Section 7.6**

This is entirely new. It defines how updates shall be handled if they contain
interfering  actions. (like ie. rate-limit 0 _and_ rate-limit 1000000 in the
same update).

I am not sure if this is relevant to ExaBGP since you do not implement a RIB. 

**Section 7.3** 

This section also makes a reference to Section 7.6 but may not relevant to
ExaBGP

All the changes are completely compatible with current implementations but fill
the gap where RFC5575 was unclear and implementations tended to behave
differently.

issue_title: Adding a new Delete Policy : SOFT_DELETE_INHERIT
issue_body:
In some case, let's say you wan't to soft delete a model, and use the own related models policy to know what to do with these related objects.

For example, with the following code:

```python
class Person(SafeDeleteModel):
    _safedelete_policy = SOFT_DELETE_INHERIT
    name = models.CharField(max_length=128)

class Group(SafeDeleteModel):
    _safedelete_policy = SOFT_DELETE_INHERIT
    name = models.CharField(max_length=128)
    members = SafeDeleteManyToManyField(Person, through='Membership')

class Membership(SafeDeleteModel):
    _safedelete_policy = HARD_DELETE
    person = models.ForeignKey(Person, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    group = models.ForeignKey(Group, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    invite_reason = models.CharField(max_length=64)
```

In that case, when you soft delete a Person, the membership is really deleted from the DB.
By the way, this could be one workaround to problem described [in this issue](https://github.com/makinacorpus/django-safedelete/issues/72).

More generally, this policy give the power back to the class itself, to determine what to do, and is more flexible that `SOFT_DELETE_CASCADE`.
It can behave exactly like `SOFT_DELETE_CASCADE` if `Person` is `SOFT_DELETE_INHERIT` and `Membership` is `SOFT_DELETE`

What do you think of this? Can you see any design flaw or potential problem with this?
For now, I tried implementing it locally and it seems to do the job.

If that sounds interesting to you, I will propose a PR.

issue_title: A resource can be created in a format different from the reality
issue_body:
## Detailed Description

Someone can create a resource in a dataset in a given format, let's say XML and point it to a resource which is of a different `Content-Type`.

For example, on https://www.data.gouv.fr/fr/datasets/resultats-de-lintegralite-des-elections-depuis-2001/, a single resource is listed as an XML file:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/138627/28375622-d71ed27e-6c9f-11e7-8ce7-c29967a05469.png)

Except it returns an HTML page with a directory of resources. There is not even an XML response type when the content is requested as XML:

```bash
$ curl --head -H 'Accept: application/xml' https://www.interieur.gouv.fr/avotreservice/elections/telechargements/
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Length: 9149
Content-Type: text/html;charset=UTF-8
...
``` 

## Context

When a content is added in a given format , I want to download a file which respects the advertised format , so I can really use it instead of looking for a needle in an FTP index.

## Possible Implementation

In the admin interface, some heuristics could prevent this kind of behaviours — and could eventually be part of healthcheck metrics.

## Your Environment

* Browser Name and version: N/A
* Operating System and version (desktop or mobile): N/A

issue_title: Pass extra args to babel functions (decimal_quantization)
issue_body:
Babel have added `decimal_quantization` to `format_decimal`, `format_currency`, `format_percent`, and `format_scientific`. Flask-Babel cannot pass this arg through when using the equivalent proxy functions so users cannot use these new features.

For reference here is Flask-Babel's `format_percent`

```python
def format_percent(number, format=None):
    """Return formatted percent value for the locale in request

    :param number: the number to format
    :param format: the format to use
    :return: the formatted percent number
    :rtype: unicode
    """
    locale = get_locale()
    return numbers.format_percent(number, format=format, locale=locale)
```

And the signature of the underlying Babel function:

```python
def format_percent(number, format=None, locale=LC_NUMERIC, decimal_quantization=True):
```

Should Flask-Babel hard code args in like this, or should it use the `*args, **kwargs` pattern to pass through extra args to the underlying Babel functions?

issue_title: Not getting changed value of class attribute in deserialized version
issue_body:
When I change a class variable defined in a module (in a file), I'm still getting the old value upon deserializing. This seems inconsistent when compared to dilling an interactively declared class, where the variable does indeed reflect the changed value. 

Say we define a class in file ```A.py```:
```
class A:
   b = 1
```
In **first** Python shell I serialize the changed class:
```
>>> from A import A
>>> A.b
1
>>> A.b = 42
>>> A.b
42
>>> with open('A.pkl', 'w') as f: dill.dump(A, f)
... 
```
In a **second** Python shell we deserialize, however the class variable retains the original value:
```
>>> with open('A.pkl', 'r') as f: A = dill.load(f)
... 
>>> A.b
1
```
Interestingly enough, everything works as expected so long as I define my class interactively. For example, in **first** Python shell we can do:
```
>>> class A:
...   b = 1
... 
>>> A.b
1
>>> A.b = 42
>>> A.b
42
>>> with open('A.pkl', 'w') as f: dill.dump(A, f)
...
```
Then in **second** Python shell, the result is as expected:
```
>>> with open('A.pkl', 'r') as f: A = dill.load(f)
...
>>> A.b
42
```

issue_title: Points generated by angular perturbation are wrong when object A is rotated
issue_body:
The following gist demonstrates that the location of contact points depends on the order of calls to `btCollisionWorld::addCollisionObject`.

https://gist.github.com/radarsat1/2eb69623b46ee10dfdb52ac617bcc658

It can be run with,

    ./collision_points_object_order | python - &

The program just sets up a collision world with a smaller box as a btConvexHullShape sitting on top of a larger box as a btBoxShape.
With current master (e87060dcf4b445c5c041933b60b9deff9ef98568) it produces the following output:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/66805/36311249-73a25246-1309-11e8-8805-8d2de7f52004.png)

Here, points on object A (i.e. getBody0) are blue, points on object B (getBody1) are red.  It seems that btConvexConvexAlgorithm correctly always selects to perturb the smaller object (the convex hull) but when it is A it generates the wrong points.  That is, they are not on the surface of either object.  I think the same points should be generated in both cases.  I think it is a wrong direction of the normal, or a wrong calculation.  I found a possible solution but am still verifying it.  I will submit a PR soon.

issue_title: Support Python 3.x
cache should check local filesystem before downloading
issue_body:
`cache.py` seems to have no code to check if a download should occur. It'd be nice if it checked if the source changed first before doing the network traffic. In many cases we can't cache; either it's a run on a new directory or it's a web service that doesn't support checks. But when debugging we could save a lot of time looking on disk. Could also turn into a system to avoid regenerating outputs if the input didn't change.

Specific proposal: add code to `download()` [here](https://github.com/openaddresses/machine/blob/26f24fa69300bd8c83db1512b0421ac1e099c7e7/openaddr/cache.py#L107-L143) to do a check against the server first. Implementing the full HTTP caching protocol is too much work, but maybe we can get by with a check on content length and date.

Looking at that code now it looks like in some circumstances the file won't be redownloaded if it exists. I haven't seen that work in practice and, even if it does, we should be smarter about checking for cache expiration.

issue_title: using Network printer and catching socket errors
issue_body:
This is very similar to issue #189, but in this case trying to catch a socket error to a network printer results in the same type of issue where the exception gets caught, but another exception happens during the garbage collection (I presume), when calling close() on the Network printer object.

Example:
```
try:
    p = printer.Network('192.168.0.100', timeout=5)
except socket.error:
    raise SystemError('error connecting to network printer')
```

If this exception is caught, another exception is raised:
```
 raise SystemError('could not connect to network printer')
File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/escpos/escpos.py", line 46, in __del__
  self.close()
File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/escpos/printer.py", line 225, in close
  self.device.shutdown(socket.SHUT_RDWR)
File "/usr/lib/python2.7/socket.py", line 228, in meth
  return getattr(self._sock,name)(*args)
socket.error: (107, 'Transport endpoint is not connected')
```

The socket isn't connected and so the shutdown command fails in the cleanup of the device.

This can be solved by adding a connected variable to the Network class and setting it to True only at the end of the open method of the printer.Network class, and then only calling the self.device.shutdown command if the self.connected variable is True. (there doesn't seem to be a good way to check if the self.device socket is connected or not?)

I'm not entirely sure if this is a good fix for this issue, but it solves the problem I was running into.

issue_title: Make Elastigroup's On Demand not optional
issue_body:
The current implementation allows users not to specify the `InstanceType` attribute which would later be translated to the Elastigroups `ondemand` attribute. [This attribute is mandatory according to the API](https://api.spotinst.com/elastigroup/amazon-web-services-2/create/#compute.instanceTypes.ondemand) and [Spotint's recommendations](https://github.com/zalando-stups/senza/pull/516#pullrequestreview-136726224). The stack would not be created.

The fallbackToOd should always be set to True given that `ondemand` is mandatory.

issue_title: Issue with choices that are translations
issue_body:
I have a model that looks something like this: 

```python
class Organisation(models.Model):

	CATEGORY_CHOICES = (
		(1, _('Government administrative authorities')),
		(2, _('Authorities under parliament')),
		(3, _('State utilities')),
		(4, _('AP fonds')),
		(5, _('Courts of Sweden and courts administration')),
		(6, _('Swedish foreign authorities')),
	)
	category = models.IntegerField(_('category'), 
		choices=CATEGORY_CHOICES
	)
```

The import part is that category is a choice that has a translation (which is in Swedish btw).
When I query for this field I get this (as an example):
"category": "STATLIGA_F_RVALTNINGSMYNDIGHETER"

...which looks pretty weird to me.

The schema looks like this:
```python
class OrganisationNode(DjangoObjectType):

	class Meta:
		model = Organisation
		interfaces = (graphene.Node,)
```
 
If I instead do this:
```python
 class OrganisationNode(DjangoObjectType):
	
	category = graphene.String()

	class Meta:
		model = Organisation
		interfaces = (graphene.Node,)
	
	def resolve_category(self, args, context, info):
		return self.get_category_display()
```
...the result is as expected, e.g.:
"category": "Statliga förvaltningsmyndigheter"

So, there is a way around it. I was just wondering if this is by design or...??

issue_title: Allow third parties to withdraw locks
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

A third party must be able to withdraw any pending locks on behalf of a participant

## Tasklist

- [ ] Make sure a lock is withdrawn only once
- [ ] If a transfer is updated the previous locksroot is probably invalidated, in that case all unlocks that happened must be rolled back and unlocked with a proof for the new locksroot.

## Related

Allow third parties to update the counterparty transfer #293
Allow third parties to close the channel #182

issue_title: SSHLibrary: sudo option is not useful when command uses redirection operators
issue_body:
The keyword  'Execute Command' from SSHLibrary library has support to execute command with sudo permissions for non-root user, Its not possible to execute complex commands e.g. commands containing redirection operator,

Execute Command       echo 0 > /etc/somefile     sudo=True    sudo_password=xxxx

issue_title: Use booleans arguments like in Robot Framework
issue_body:
RF convenience methods for handling booleans, example 
```
| Some Keyword | False |
```
Is evaluated as False in Robot Framework standard libraries, although the argument is string and according Python it should evaluate as True. Changing the S2L to behave in similar fashion as the Robot Framework standard libraries could be useful.

More details in http://robotframework.org/robotframework/latest/libraries/BuiltIn.html#Boolean%20arguments and some discussion in #717

issue_title: OrderedContainer removes the key from _order if an event listener throws an exception, but not _data
issue_body:
And because `.keys()` returns _order, this can lead to the case where .keys() and `in`, etc, are out of sync.

There is a test (test_exception_causes_order_fi) for the first half of this (that an exception removes the key from .keys()) but not the second half.

Because none of the other containers remove the key if there is an exception in setitem running the event listeners, I consider this a bug in OrderedContainer.

issue_title: Caffe converter for convolution mapping error for 'withBias'
issue_body:
'withBias' is an optional param in 'SpatialConvotion' layer and previously there was no other params between 'group' and 'withBias'  and the Converter created convolution layer from Caffe as follows :

Seq(SpatialConvolution[T](nInputPlane.toInt, nOutPlane.toInt,
          kw, kh, dw, dh, pw, ph, group,  withBias).setName(getLayerName(layer)).inputs())

we recently refined the layer by adding more params which caused withBias wrongly pointed to another param, we should explicitly specify the para as 

  Seq(SpatialConvolution[T](nInputPlane.toInt, nOutPlane.toInt,
          kw, kh, dw, dh, pw, ph, group, withBias = withBias).setName(getLayerName(layer)).inputs())

issue_title: Use whole segments instead of prefix when matching for active
issue_body:
Currently the menu item is marked as active when there is a prefix match on the URL. This creates situations where multiple different menu items appear to be active just because they share a prefix. The match should happen against complete segments within the path.

For example:

* _/object_
* _/objects_

Currently, if I navigate to _/object_, both of the above menuitems will have _active_ set to _True_ which is not intuitive.

issue_title: Add caching mechanism
issue_body:
Originally by @femtotrader on https://github.com/trickvi/datapackage/issues/61:

> Hello,
> 
> I think datapackage should provide a cache mechanism.
> 
> For this (if user want to have this cache mechanism 2 optional dependencies could be requests and requests-cache)
> 
> * http://requests.readthedocs.org/en/latest/
> * http://requests-cache.readthedocs.org/
> I don't suggest to monkey-patch requests to use CachedSession by default but to pass a CachedSession instead of a requests.Session
> 
> One possible use could be :
> ```python
> import datapackage
> import requests_cache
> import datetime
> session = requests_cache.CachedSession(cache_name='cache', backend='sqlite', expire_after=datetime.timedelta(days=60))
> datapkg = datapackage.DataPackage('http://data.okfn.org/data/cpi/', session=session)
> ```
> Default value of parameter session should beNone`.
> This session should be stored as a member of DataPackage.
> 
> When session is not None request will be performed using
> 
> ```
> self.session.get(url)
> ```
> Kind regards
> 
> PS : a similar approach was used in https://github.com/femtotrader/pandas_datareaders_unofficial
> 
> edit: and is now (oct 2015) used in official "pandas-datareader" https://github.com/pydata/pandas-datareader/
> 
> see also pydata/pandas-datareader#48

issue_title: add kolibri command to change the location of the content folder
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

On a number of occasions, we've gotten feedback that folks want the content folder in a different location than the primary Kolibri directory. This often occurs even after a user has imported content, which means that we may need to migrate existing content to the new locations.

### Expected behavior

We should provide a new command to migrate the content directory to a new location. One possibility might be something like:

```bash
kolibri content directory  # displays current directory
kolibri content directory /media/external  # migrates the content to a new directory
```

However any command is fine if it serves the general purpose.

It would be reasonable to place some restrictions on this command if helpful, e.g. that the Kolibri server can't be running or be started while it's running.

This may also require some additional checks during Kolibri startup, since now there would be a chance that Kolibri might start without the content directory being available, or the content directory might become unavailable while Kolibri is running. These cases might require errors to get bubbled up to the end-user on the front-end.

### User-facing consequences

Users are not able to set up their systems and leverage higher-capacity hard drives as desired

issue_title: Correctly use fullscreen modals & action bars
issue_body:
The style guide specs out a fullscreen modal and action bar components. To summarize, fullscreen modals should be overlays without a unique url. If there is a unique url, then action bars should be used. This does not call for an overlay, just a change in action bar, from the default app bar to the appropriate action bar.

Currently there are some places, such as the permissions page, that use the fullscreen modal component, aka `immersive-fullscreen`, but should in fact be just using the action bars

Fullscreen modal:
https://docs.google.com/document/d/15Y1RDzsO_vpRSSEtL_L5lCzZtAtN4j3qN813_bnL4uw/edit?usp=drive_web

Action bars:

issue_title: Schema generator for data classes
issue_body:
Python 3.7 will come with a new feature called Data Classes (see [PEP 557](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0557/)). It is available for Python 3.6 on PyPI for demonstration purposes.

It looks like this:
```python
@dataclass
class Artist:
    name: str

@dataclass
class Album:
    title: str
    release_date: datetime.date
    artist: List[Artist]
```

You can immediately notice the resemblance with marshmallow:
```python
class ArtistSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.Str()

class AlbumSchema(Schema):
    title = fields.Str()
    release_date = fields.Date()
    artist = fields.Nested(ArtistSchema())
```

I think for most cases there's enough information in the dataclass for marshmallow to figure out the schemas by itself. I admit that the schema might change while the model will stay the same but for most cases, it won't be a problem.

My idea is to leverage on the dataclass to build a Schema automatically for DRY purposes when it makes sense while still getting the features of marshmallow under the hood. What's your opinion on that? Do you have an idea of implementation so it can be both DRY and extensible?

I may be able to work on a PR for this but I want to go in the right direction.

issue_title: In case of network error it may happend that bubuku won't start
issue_body:
If unhandled exception will occur in run_daemon_loop cycle, that bubuku process will try to restart it's own work cycle, and recreate all internal entities (having in mind existing kafka process).
But if in that case AmazonEnvProvder.get_region() will return null, than it won't be able to refresh exhibitor address list, and that will start unlimited cycle of exceptions (see AWSExhibitorAddressProvider.get_addresses_by_lb_name).

issue_title: Proxies in django polymorphic
issue_body:
Proxies in Django  are usually used for separating admin interface for some subsets of objects

let's take django-polymorphic pexp example 
I'd like to have Projects to be grouped in separate admin, so, additionally to pexp example define

in models.py
```
class ProjectProxy(Project):
    class Meta:
        proxy = True
```
in admin.py
```
class ProjectProxyAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    model = ProjectProxy
admin.site.register(ProjectProxy, ProjectProxyAdmin)
```
after creating
ProxyProject objects are safe in separate admin,
ProxyProject objects are also listed in Project admin,
but when they are clicked - there is 403 Forbidden message.

now it is possible to add ProxyProject  to
```
class ProjectAdmin(PolymorphicParentModelAdmin):
    base_model = Project
    list_filter = (PolymorphicChildModelFilter,)
    child_models = (Project, ArtProject, ResearchProject,ProjectProxy)
```
but then - ProjectProxy is listed in admin/pexp/project/add/ types,  but is absolutely not needed here - if I want to have new Project objects - it does not mean I want to clutter the admin interface with spurious proxy objects

so is it possible to have either

1) not raise forbidden for objects in Projects at /admin/pexp/project/ ( if model is not listed as child_models,but for proxy it's base is in child models)
2) ability to explicitly exclude models registered child_models from a list of available models in admin/pexp/project/add/

the second option can be achieved by  having PolymorphicParentModelAdmin field skipProxies
and then in def get_child_type_choices(self, request, action):
have
```
if skipProxies and model._meta.proxy == True:
                continue
```
P.S. in above illustrative example it makes no sense to have all Projects in both Proxy admin and in normal polymorphic admin, but it really makes sense if a desire is  to place all ArtProject objects in a separate admin:
```
class ArtProjectProxy(ArtProject):
    class Meta:
        proxy = True

class ArtProjectProxyAdmin(admin.ModelAdmin):
    model = ArtProjectProxy
    def get_queryset(self, request):
        return ArtProject.objects.all()
```
still - it will cause above mentioned problems ( 403 error or list of additional ArtProjectProxy in polymorphic admin )

issue_title: IPython 6.0+ only supports Python 3+
issue_body:
Context: http://blog.jupyter.org/2017/04/19/release-of-ipython-6-0/#sunsettingpython2support

The `update_requirements` command is not smart enough (and I'm not sure how we would make it smart enough) to figure out that:

- Given a requirements.txt intended for Python 2 (which we have no way of knowing), we should update ipython to the latest 5.x, but no higher.
- Given a requirements.txt intended for Python 3, we should update ipython (any version) to the latest ipython, which is 6.x or above.

issue_title: google-cloud-trace wheels are **not** universal
issue_body:
This is because of a missing `trace/setup.cfg`. For example, in the latest [release][1] the file uploaded corresponds to the Python that built the wheel: `google_cloud_trace-0.16.0-py3-none-any.whl` (it should end with `-py2.py3-none-any.whl`.

@lukesneeringer I am assigning to you because I think this is a codegen issue. There is a simple fix to add a two line file `setup.cfg`:

```
[bdist_wheel]
universal = 1
```

[1]: https://pypi.org/project/google-cloud-trace/0.16.0/

issue_body:
In my packed-refs file, following contents:
```
# pack-refs with: peeled fully-peeled sorted 
a5052c65b74b79851e9b91589a83906deb434b8a refs/heads/master
a9a4140068e7108a57cf038c2864def8e27f06d9 refs/heads/test
a5052c65b74b79851e9b91589a83906deb434b8a refs/remotes/origin/commit_test

df5227b1e584215e9f69a577b7b284230b5a5e1d refs/tags/테스트
^a9a4140068e7108a57cf038c2864def8e27f06d9
```
problem encounter from `df5227b1e584215e9f69a577b7b284230b5a5e1d refs/tags/테스트`,
and error message here:

```Python
  File "C:\Users\admin\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36\lib\site-packages\git\repo\base.py", line 269, in heads
    return Head.list_items(self)
  File "C:\Users\admin\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36\lib\site-packages\git\util.py", line 934, in list_items
    out_list.extend(cls.iter_items(repo, *args, **kwargs))
  File "C:\Users\admin\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36\lib\site-packages\git\refs\symbolic.py", line 617, in _iter_items
    for sha, rela_path in cls._iter_packed_refs(repo):  # @UnusedVariable
  File "C:\Users\admin\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python36\lib\site-packages\git\refs\symbolic.py", line 94, in _iter_packed_refs
    for line in fp:
UnicodeDecodeError: 'cp949' codec can't decode byte 0xed in position 485: illegal multibyte sequence
```
(This is not common case of naming for branch or tags name as non-english. but, I need handing for this problem.)

problem point is line no.94 at `_iter_packed_refs` in `symbolic.py`.
```Python
line 93:with open(osp.join(repodir, ref_path), 'rt') as fp:
line 94:  value = fp.read().rstrip()
```
when open as `fp`, properties has
```Python
 fp: <_io.TextIOWrapper name='some\\path\\.git\\packed-refs' mode='rt' encoding='cp949'>
```
and next line `value = fp.read().rstrip()`, this builtin lib can not read non-unicode string
I think, this expected encoding is `'utf-8'` or `'unicode'`, but is setting for default system language encoder.

So, I modified this line(no.93 and 148 at symbolic.py)  for that
```python
#with open(cls._get_packed_refs_path(repo), 'rt') as fp:
#modify: encoding option setting for multilanguage
with open(cls._get_packed_refs_path(repo), 'rt', encoding='utf-8') as fp:
```

after this modify, it has no problem! but, I worried about some side-effect.
few source code used yet `open()` function, I can not certain for every coverage from os, encoding system and etc.

please check this problem.
